column4: Question
column6: 1 - Lagging
column7: 2 - Developing
column8: 3 - Proficient
column9: 4 - Exemplary

column4: How effectively does the head establish and maintain consistent design delivery schedules?
column6: Design sprints and delivery timelines are frequently missed or undefined. Team lacks visibility into design deadlines and expectations are unclear.
column7: Design schedules exist but are inconsistent; some slippage occurs regularly. Team has partial visibility into upcoming deliverables and timelines.
column8: Design cadence is predictable and reliable with clear sprint goals. Team can anticipate design deliverables and maintains consistent timelines.
column9: Design cadence is exemplary with proactive planning that adapts to business needs. Team reliably delivers designs on schedule while maintaining flexibility for high-priority work.

column4: To what extent does the head maintain design quality standards while ensuring consistency across deliverables?
column6: Design outputs vary significantly in quality and consistency; brand guidelines are poorly followed. Design system components lack uniformity and updates are haphazard.
column7: Design quality is generally acceptable but inconsistent across projects. Brand guidelines are followed with occasional lapses; design system updates are infrequent.
column8: Design quality is consistently high with strong adherence to brand guidelines and design system. Components are well-maintained and updates are timely and coordinated.
column9: Design quality is exceptional with flawless consistency across all deliverables. Design system is a reference standard; all components are optimized for reusability and clearly documented.

column4: How effectively does the head allocate team resources and manage workload distribution?
column6: Team workload is unevenly distributed with some members overloaded while others are underutilized. Resource allocation is reactive and causes frequent burnout or bottlenecks.
column7: Workload distribution is mostly reasonable but occasional imbalances occur. Team members have some periods of overload but generally sustainable pace.
column8: Team workload is well-balanced with clear allocation strategies. Resources are matched to projects based on skills and capacity; team maintains healthy pace.
column9: Workload is optimally distributed with proactive capacity planning and skill-based assignment. Team maintains high engagement and performance while preventing burnout and skill silos.

column4: To what extent does the head evaluate and optimize design tools and advocate for process improvements?
column6: Design tools are outdated or suboptimal; Figma and Adobe Suite usage lacks standardization. Process improvements are rarely proposed or implemented.
column7: Design tool stack is acceptable but not fully optimized; some standardization across Figma and Adobe Suite exists. Occasional process improvements are suggested but inconsistently implemented.
column8: Design tools are well-optimized and standardized; Figma libraries and Adobe workflows are efficient. Process improvements are regularly evaluated and implemented.
column9: Design tools are cutting-edge and fully integrated; Figma component libraries and Adobe Suite workflows are exemplary. Head continuously drives innovation and optimization across the design stack.

column4: How effectively does the head ensure design deliverables are complete and ready for handoff?
column6: Design deliverables are frequently incomplete or lack necessary specifications and handoff documentation. Handoffs to development are often delayed or problematic.
column7: Design deliverables are mostly complete but occasionally missing specs or annotations. Handoffs generally occur on schedule with minor issues.
column8: Design deliverables are thorough and complete with clear specs and documentation. Handoffs are smooth and development teams receive all necessary assets.
column9: Design deliverables are exemplary with comprehensive specifications, design tokens, and documentation. Handoffs are seamless; development integrates designs with minimal back-and-forth.

column4: To what extent does the head communicate design progress and address stakeholder concerns?
column6: Design status is rarely communicated; stakeholders are uncertain about progress. Design team is unresponsive to stakeholder feedback and concerns.
column7: Design status updates are provided intermittently; stakeholders have incomplete visibility. Stakeholder feedback is acknowledged but responses are sometimes delayed.
column8: Design status is regularly communicated with clear progress updates. Stakeholder concerns are addressed promptly and feedback loops are established.
column9: Design communication is proactive and transparent with regular status briefings and executive summaries. Stakeholders feel heard and informed; feedback loops are strong and collaborative.

column3: Design Issue Response & Root Cause Analysis
column4: How effectively does the head identify and resolve design issues and systemic problems?
column6: Design issues are addressed slowly or superficially without root cause analysis. Recurring problems are not analyzed or prevented.
column7: Design issues are addressed but root cause analysis is inconsistent. Some recurring problems are identified but prevention strategies are weak.
column8: Design issues are investigated and addressed with clear root cause analysis. Prevention strategies are implemented to reduce recurring problems.
column9: Design issues are rapidly identified and thoroughly analyzed with proactive root cause elimination. Prevention strategies are exemplary; systemic problems are rare.

column4: To what extent does the head enforce design review standards and ensure readiness before implementation?
column6: Design reviews are inconsistent or skipped; designs proceed to implementation without adequate review. Quality gates are weak or non-existent.
column7: Design reviews occur but are inconsistent in rigor or completeness. Some designs bypass review or move forward prematurely.
column8: Design reviews are structured and consistent; designs receive appropriate feedback before implementation. Quality gates are clear and enforced.
column9: Design reviews are exemplary with rigorous, constructive feedback that improves outcomes. Quality gates are strong; designs are exceptionally well-prepared for implementation.

column4: How effectively does the head remove obstacles and maintain smooth design workflow?
column6: Design workflow is frequently blocked by tools
column7: handoffs
column8: or dependencies. Blockers are not prioritized and cause cascading delays.
column9: Design workflow experiences occasional blockers; some are resolved but resolution can be slow. Handoff dependencies sometimes create delays.
column10: Design workflow is smooth with most blockers identified and resolved quickly. Interdependencies are well-managed and handoffs are coordinated.
column11: Design workflow is exceptionally fluid with proactive blocker identification and rapid removal. Dependencies are anticipated and handoffs are seamless.

column4: How effectively does the head maintain and evolve the design system and documentation?
column6: Design system is incomplete or outdated; documentation is lacking or poorly organized. Design tokens and component standards are undefined.
column7: Design system exists but has gaps in coverage or outdated components. Documentation is partially complete; design tokens need better definition.
column8: Design system is well-maintained with current components and clear documentation. Design tokens are defined; documentation is accessible and comprehensive.
column9: Design system is exemplary and a reference standard across the organization. Documentation is exceptional; design tokens are optimized and frequently referenced.

column4: To what extent does the head foster design excellence through reviews and mentorship?
column6: Design reviews provide minimal feedback; peer mentorship is absent or ineffective. Junior designers receive little guidance or growth opportunities.
column7: Design reviews occur but feedback is sometimes inconsistent. Peer mentorship happens informally; junior designers receive limited structured guidance.
column8: Design reviews provide constructive feedback that strengthens work. Peer mentorship is structured and supports junior designer growth.
column9: Design reviews are exceptional with deep feedback that elevates team capabilities. Mentorship is proactive and deliberate; junior designers show accelerated growth.

column4: How effectively does the head identify and reduce design debt?
column6: Design debt is not tracked or addressed; technical design issues accumulate. Design system and components become increasingly difficult to maintain.
column7: Design debt is acknowledged but not systematically tracked. Some efforts to reduce debt occur but are inconsistent.
column8: Design debt is tracked and actively managed; regular efforts to reduce legacy issues. Maintenance burden is reasonable.
column9: Design debt is minimal and proactively managed with continuous improvement. Design system remains lean and maintainable.

column4: To what extent does the head facilitate team learning and skill development?
column6: Knowledge sharing is minimal; team learns in silos. Skill development opportunities are rare or unavailable.
column7: Knowledge sharing occurs informally; team has some skill development opportunities. Learning is inconsistent across team members.
column8: Knowledge sharing is structured through regular sessions and documentation. Team skill development is supported through training and varied project exposure.
column9: Knowledge sharing is exemplary with intentional learning culture; team participates in cross-functional projects and external development. All team members continuously evolve skills.

column4: To what extent does the head create an environment where team members feel safe to contribute and experiment?
column6: Team does not feel safe to voice concerns or ideas. Design culture is risk-averse and discourages experimentation.
column7: Team feels mostly comfortable but some members hesitate to contribute ideas. Experimentation is permitted but not actively encouraged.
column8: Team experiences psychological safety with open idea-sharing and feedback. Constructive failure is acceptable and learning-oriented.
column9: Psychological safety is exceptional; team members are highly engaged and contribute freely. Culture of experimentation is strong with risk-taking and learning celebrated.

column4: How effectively does the head maintain team stability and engagement?
column6: Team turnover is high; engagement and morale are low. Team members express frustration or lack of fulfillment.
column7: Team retention is acceptable but some high-performers leave. Engagement is moderate; satisfaction is mixed.
column8: Team retention is strong with good engagement levels. Most team members report satisfaction and commitment.
column9: Team retention is exemplary with high engagement and strong loyalty. Team members are fulfilled and ambitious about design impact.

column4: How effectively does the head capture design knowledge and share it across divisions?
column6: Design knowledge is not documented or shared beyond the team. Insights remain siloed within the department.
column7: Design knowledge is partially captured and occasionally shared. Cross-division collaboration is limited.
column8: Design knowledge is documented and regularly shared with other teams. Cross-division collaboration occurs; best practices are disseminated.
column9: Design knowledge is systematically captured and actively shared across the organization. Design standards and insights inform enterprise decisions.

column4: To what extent does the head articulate design vision and align the team to shared purpose?
column6: Design vision is unclear or unshared; team lacks clear sense of purpose. Decisions feel disconnected from broader goals.
column7: Design vision is communicated but not consistently understood. Team has partial alignment on purpose and direction.
column8: Design vision is clear and team understands how their work supports it. Shared purpose guides prioritization and decisions.
column9: Design vision is compelling and inspires team commitment. Team is unified around purpose; all work clearly connects to strategic goals.

column4: How effectively does the head drive strategic design initiatives and measure their impact?
column6: Strategic initiatives are absent or poorly defined; design impact is not measured. Business value of design work is unclear.
column7: Strategic initiatives exist but measurement of impact is inconsistent. Design influence on business outcomes is limited.
column8: Strategic initiatives are well-defined with clear impact metrics. Design contributions to business outcomes are measurable.
column9: Strategic initiatives are exemplary with strong business impact and ROI. Design influences key business decisions; impact is continuously measured and communicated.

column4: How effectively does the head prioritize design work based on risk and impact?
column6: Prioritization is unclear or reactive; high-risk design issues are not addressed. Design strategy lacks risk assessment framework.
column7: Prioritization considers some factors but risk assessment is inconsistent. Some high-impact work is prioritized; others are overlooked.
column8: Prioritization is systematic with clear risk and impact assessment. High-risk and high-impact design work receives appropriate focus.
column9: Prioritization is exemplary with sophisticated risk modeling and impact analysis. Design resources are optimally allocated to maximize business value.

column4: To what extent does the head explain design trade-offs and facilitate informed decision-making?
column6: Trade-offs are not articulated; stakeholders lack understanding of design decisions. Scope negotiations are opaque or frustrating.
column7: Trade-offs are acknowledged but explanations are sometimes vague. Stakeholders have partial understanding of design choices.
column8: Trade-offs are clearly articulated with reasoning. Stakeholders understand scope and design compromises; decisions are informed.
column9: Trade-offs are expertly communicated with scenarios and implications. Stakeholders feel informed and confident in design decisions.

column4: How effectively does the head respond to feedback and maintain communication channels?
column6: Feedback is not solicited or acted upon; communication channels are unclear. Team and stakeholders feel unheard.
column7: Feedback is collected but responses are slow. Communication channels exist but are not always effective.
column8: Feedback is consistently solicited and addressed; responsive communication is maintained. Team and stakeholders feel heard.
column9: Feedback loops are exemplary and transparent; responsiveness is rapid and constructive. Communication channels are highly effective.

column4: How effectively does the head implement automation to enhance design efficiency?
column6: Design automation is minimal; repetitive work is manual. Figma and Adobe workflows lack optimization.
column7: Design automation is emerging; some tools and scripts reduce manual work. Workflows show signs of optimization.
column8: Design automation is well-implemented; Figma components, design tokens, and scripts reduce manual effort. Efficiency gains are measurable.
column9: Design automation is exemplary and enterprise-grade; Figma libraries and Adobe workflows are fully optimized. Automation frees team for strategic work.

column4: To what extent does the head address team skill gaps and ensure capability growth?
column6: Skill gaps are not identified; team lacks critical capabilities. Training and development are unavailable.
column7: Skill gaps are identified but addressed inconsistently. Some training is provided; capability growth is slow.
column8: Skill gaps are regularly assessed and addressed through training. Team capabilities are systematically strengthened.
column9: Skill gaps are proactively identified and closed through tailored development. Team capabilities are continuously elevated and future-ready.

column4: How effectively does the head lead design transformation and organizational change?
column6: Design evolution is reactive and slow; team resists change. Change initiatives lack leadership and fail.
column7: Design evolution occurs but implementation is inconsistent. Change leadership is present but sometimes ineffective.
column8: Design evolution is managed with clear strategy and team alignment. Change initiatives are well-led and implemented.
column9: Design evolution is exemplary with visionary leadership; team embraces change and drives innovation. Transformation is seamless and impactful.

column4: How effectively does the head empower team members with autonomy and decision-making authority?
column6: Team lacks autonomy; decision-making is overly centralized. Team members feel micromanaged and constrained.
column7: Team has some autonomy but significant decisions require approval. Empowerment is inconsistent across team members.
column8: Team members are empowered with clear decision-making authority. Autonomy is balanced with guidance and accountability.
column9: Team empowerment is exemplary; designers have significant authority and ownership. Trust and autonomy drive high performance and accountability.

column3: Culture of Experimentation & Creative Problem Solving
column4: To what extent does the head foster a culture where creative problem-solving and experimentation are valued?
column6: Experimentation is discouraged; design solutions are conventional. Creative problem-solving is not prioritized.
column7: Experimentation is tolerated but not actively encouraged. Creative approaches occasionally emerge but are inconsistent.
column8: Experimentation is encouraged and supported; team explores creative solutions. Design work reflects diverse problem-solving approaches.
column9: Experimentation is exemplary; team actively explores novel solutions and creative approaches. Design outcomes are innovative and impactful.
